content:
# 1 Timothy Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TI_001_page_001.jpg

1. Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by command of God our Savior and of Christ Jesus our hope,

2. To Timothy, my true child in the faith: Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

3. As I urged you when I was going to Macedonia, remain at Ephesus so that you may charge certain persons not to teach any different doctrine,

4. nor to devote themselves to myths and endless genealogies, which promote speculations rather than the stewardship from God that is by faith.

5. The aim of our charge is love that issues from a pure heart and a good conscience and a sincere faith.

6. Certain persons, by swerving from these, have wandered away into vain discussion,

7. desiring to be teachers of the law, without understanding either what they are saying or the things about which they make confident assertions.

8. Now we know that the law is good, if one uses it lawfully,

9. understanding this, that the law is not laid down for the just but for the lawless and disobedient, for the ungodly and sinners, for the unholy and profane, for those who strike their fathers and mothers, for murderers,

10. the sexually immoral, men who practice homosexuality, enslavers, liars, perjurers, and whatever else is contrary to sound doctrine,

11. in accordance with the gospel of the glory of the blessed God with which I have been entrusted.

12. I thank him who has given me strength, Christ Jesus our Lord, because he judged me faithful, appointing me to his service,

13. though formerly I was a blasphemer, persecutor, and insolent opponent. But I received mercy because I had acted ignorantly in unbelief,

14. and the grace of our Lord overflowed for me with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus.

15. The saying is trustworthy and deserving of full acceptance, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost.

16. But I received mercy for this reason, that in me, as the foremost, Jesus Christ might display his perfect patience as an example to those who were to believe in him for eternal life.

17. To the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.

18. This charge I entrust to you, Timothy, my child, in accordance with the prophecies previously made about you, that by them you may wage the good warfare,

19. holding faith and a good conscience. By rejecting this, some have made shipwreck of their faith,

20. among whom are Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom I have handed over to Satan that they may learn not to blaspheme.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1 Timothy is the first of the Pastoral Epistles — letters addressed to individual church leaders rather than congregations. The letter addresses false teaching (ch.1), church order (chs.2-3), Timothy's personal ministry (ch.4), and specific pastoral situations (chs.5-6). The "faithful sayings" (pistos ho logos) are a distinctive feature of the Pastorals.

**Verse 15 — The first faithful saying:** All three translators preserved the confessional formula. SOPHIA affirms: "Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners" is a community confession — compressed gospel in one clause.

**Verse 17 — The doxology:** All three translators preserved the four divine attributes. SOPHIA affirms: this is a Jewish doxological form adapted for Christian worship.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.4 — "the administration/stewardship of God" vs. "a life of faith in God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the administration of God which [is] in faith" | oikonomian theou tēn en pistei |
| LOGOS | "the stewardship from God that is by faith" | "Stewardship" for oikonomia |
| PNEUMA | "don't help people live a life of faith in God" | Drops oikonomia entirely; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. oikonomia theou is "the stewardship/plan of God" — a technical Pauline term for God's redemptive economy (cf. Eph 1:10, 3:2). PNEUMA drops the term entirely, replacing it with "live a life of faith in God" — losing the concept of God's redemptive plan.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses oikonomia concept.

---

### v.5 — "the goal of the commandment" vs. "the aim of our charge" vs. "the purpose of my instruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the goal of the commandment is love" | to de telos tēs parangelias estin agapē |
| LOGOS | "The aim of our charge is love" | "Charge" for parangelia |
| PNEUMA | "The purpose of my instruction is that all believers would be filled with love" | "My instruction" — personalizes; "all believers would be filled with" — adds |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says the telos (goal/end) of the parangelia (charge/command) is agapē (love). PNEUMA personalizes ("my instruction") and adds "all believers would be filled with" — expansion not in Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA personalizes and expands.

---

### v.10 — "arsenokoitais" — translation of sexual terminology

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "men who lie with men" | arsenokoitais — compound: arsēn (male) + koitē (bed) |
| LOGOS | "men who practice homosexuality" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "who practice homosexuality" | Same |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. arsenokoitēs is a compound word likely derived from LXX Leviticus 18:22 / 20:13 (arsenos koitēn). EMET's literal rendering ("men who lie with men") preserves the etymological components. LOGOS and PNEUMA use the modern category "homosexuality" — which maps imperfectly onto the ancient term. All renderings are defensible; LOGOS is adopted for consistency.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Note: the scope and precise referent of arsenokoitēs remains debated in scholarship.

---

### v.13 — "being ignorant I acted in unbelief" vs. "I did it in ignorance and unbelief" vs. "I used to blaspheme the name of Christ. In my insolence, I persecuted his people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "but I was shown mercy, because being ignorant I acted in unbelief" | agnoōn epoiēsa en apistia |
| LOGOS | "But I received mercy because I had acted ignorantly in unbelief" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I used to blaspheme the name of Christ. In my insolence, I persecuted his people. But God had mercy on me because I did it in ignorance and unbelief" | Adds "the name of Christ," "his people," "God" as subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "the name of Christ" (Paul says just "blasphemer"), "his people" (Paul says just "persecutor"), and makes "God" the explicit subject where Paul uses a passive ("I was shown mercy"). The additions are interpretively correct but expand the compressed text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands compressed autobiography.

---

### v.14 — "the grace of our Lord overflowed exceedingly" vs. "Oh, how generous and gracious our Lord was!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the grace of our Lord overflowed exceedingly with faith and love" | hyperepleonasen hē charis |
| LOGOS | "the grace of our Lord overflowed for me with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus" | Same — preserves "overflowed" |
| PNEUMA | "Oh, how generous and gracious our Lord was! He filled me with the faith and love that come from Christ Jesus" | "Oh, how generous and gracious" — exclamatory; splits into two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul uses hyperepleonasen — "super-abounded/overflowed exceedingly." PNEUMA replaces this with an exclamation ("Oh, how generous and gracious!") — losing the specific verb of overflow and adding an emotional interjection.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the overflow verb.

---

### v.17 — "King of the ages" vs. "the eternal King"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, [the] only God" | basilei tōn aiōnōn |
| LOGOS | "the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "He is the eternal King, the unseen one who never dies; there is only one God" | "Eternal King" for "King of the ages"; "unseen one who never dies" — paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "King of the ages" (basileus tōn aiōnōn) is a Jewish doxological title — God who reigns over all epochs. PNEUMA changes to "eternal King" — shifting from God's sovereignty over historical periods to a general attribute. PNEUMA also expands "immortal, invisible" into "the unseen one who never dies" — a paraphrase that loses the compressed doxological form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the "ages" concept and expands compressed doxology.

---

### v.19 — "having thrust away" vs. "by rejecting this" vs. "have deliberately violated their consciences"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "which some, having thrust away, made shipwreck concerning the faith" | apōsamenoi — "having thrust away" |
| LOGOS | "By rejecting this, some have made shipwreck of their faith" | "Rejecting" for apōsamenoi |
| PNEUMA | "some people have deliberately violated their consciences; as a result, their faith has been shipwrecked" | Adds "deliberately"; "violated their consciences" — restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says some "thrust away" (apōtheomai) their good conscience, resulting in faith-shipwreck. PNEUMA adds "deliberately" (not in Greek) and changes "thrust away" to "violated" — a different action.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "deliberately" and changes verb.

---

### v.20 — "handed over to the Adversary" vs. "handed over to Satan" vs. "threw them out and handed them over to Satan"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whom I handed over to the Adversary" | paredōka tō satana |
| LOGOS | "whom I have handed over to Satan" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I threw them out and handed them over to Satan so they might learn not to blaspheme God" | Adds "I threw them out and"; adds "God" after "blaspheme" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "I threw them out" (excommunication is implied but not stated) and "God" after "blaspheme" (Paul just says "not to blaspheme" — the object is unspecified).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds implied actions and specifies object.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 4 | "All believers filled with" (v.5), "the name of Christ/his people" (v.13), "deliberately" (v.19), "threw them out/God" (v.20) |
| PNEUMA loses technical terms | 1 | oikonomia dropped (v.4) |
| PNEUMA paraphrase of doxology | 1 | "King of the ages" → "eternal King" (v.17) |
| PNEUMA restructures | 2 | Exclamatory rewrite (v.14), conscience violation (v.19) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 1's most significant disagreement is PNEUMA's dropping of oikonomia theou (v.4) — the same technical Pauline term seen in Ephesians 1:10 and 3:2. The concept of God's redemptive economy/stewardship is central to Pauline theology. PNEUMA also restructures the doxology (v.17), replacing "King of the ages" with "eternal King" — losing the Jewish concept of God's sovereignty over historical epochs in favor of a general eternity attribute.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
